content: Consider the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{\cos ^2 \alpha}+\frac{y^2}{\sin ^2 \alpha}=1$, where $\alpha \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$. Then, locus of point of intersection of one of the directrix and tangent at upper end of minor axis is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { From the given ellipse, }\\ &\begin{aligned} \sin ^2 \alpha & =\cos ^2 \alpha\left(1-e^2\right) \\ e & =\sqrt{1-\tan ^2 \alpha} \\ \text { and } \quad x & =\frac{a}{e} \Rightarrow h=\frac{\cos \alpha}{\sqrt{1-\tan ^2 \alpha}} \\ y & =b \Rightarrow k=\sin \alpha \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { So, } h=\frac{\sqrt{1-k^2}}{\sqrt{1-\frac{k^2}{\left(1-k^2\right)}}}=\frac{\left(1-k^2\right)}{\sqrt{1-2 k^2}} \\ & \Rightarrow h^2=\frac{\left(1-k^2\right)^2}{\left(1-2 k^2\right)} \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the locus of required point $x^2\left(1-2 y^2\right)=\left(1-y^2\right)^2$

content: If a man running around a race-course notes that the sum of the distances of two flag-posts from him is always $$10 \mathrm{~m}$$ and the distance between the flag-posts is $$8 \mathrm{~m}$$, then the area of the path he encloses in square metres is
explanation:
Let $$P(x, y)$$ be the position of the man at any time.

Let $$S(4,0)$$ and $$S^{\prime}(-4,0)$$ be the fixed flag-posts with $$C$$ as the origin. Since, $$S P+S^{\prime} P=10 \mathrm{~m}$$ i.e. a constant, then the locus of $$P$$ is an ellipse with $$S$$ and $$S^{\prime}$$ as foci and the length of the major axis is $$10 \mathrm{~m}$$.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow \quad a e=4 \text { and } 2 a=10 \Rightarrow e=\frac{4}{5} \\ \Rightarrow \quad b^2=a^2\left(1-e^2\right) \\ \qquad b^2=25\left(1-\frac{16}{25}\right)=9 \Rightarrow b=3 \end{array}$$

Hence, the area of the ellipse $$=\pi a b$$

$$=\pi \times 5 \times 3=15 \pi$$

content: The focal distance of the point $$(x, y)$$ from the ellipse $$\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1, a>b$$ is
explanation:
We know that, the focal distance or radii of the point $$(x, y)$$ from the ellipse $$\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1,(a>b)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =a \pm e x \\ \Rightarrow \quad & a \pm \sqrt{\frac{a^2-b^2}{a^2}} \cdot x=a \pm \sqrt{1-\frac{b^2}{a^2}} \cdot x \end{aligned}$$

content: The eccentricity of the ellipse $$25 x^2+9 y^2-150 x-90 y+225=0$$ is
explanation:
Given curve is

$$\begin{aligned} & 25 x^2+9 y^2-150 x-90 y+225=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \left(25 x^2-150 x\right)+\left(9 y^2-90 y\right)+225=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 25\left(x^2-6 x\right)+9\left(y^2-10 y\right)+225=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 25\left\{x^2-6 x+9-9\right\}+9\left\{y^2-10 y+25-25\right\}+225=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 25(x-3)^2-225+9(y-5)^2-225+225=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 25(x-3)^2+9(y-5)^2=225 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{(x-3)^2}{9}+\frac{(y-5)^2}{25}=1 \quad[\because(b>a)] \end{aligned}$$

Which represent an ellipse.

Whose eccentricity

$$e=\sqrt{\frac{b^2-a^2}{b^2}}=\sqrt{\frac{25-9}{25}}=\sqrt{\frac{16}{25}}=\frac{4}{5}$$
